paper_title: Can Generalist Agents Automate Data Curation?

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Curating training data is among the most consequential yet labor-intensive parts of modern AI development: practitioners iteratively propose, implement, evaluate, and revise data policies against noisy benchmark feedback. We ask whether generalist coding agents can automate this data-curation loop. We introduce *Curation-Bench*, an agent-centric benchmark that fixes the model, training recipe, and evaluation suite while giving agents command-line access to inspect data, implement policies, submit them to a fixed training/evaluation pipeline, and revise. In a vision-language instruction-tuning instantiation, out-of-the-box agents reach strong published data-selection baselines within ten iterations. However, trajectory analysis reveals a persistent *execution-research gap*: agents mainly tune local policy variants rather than explore new policy families, even when given strategy guides and paper references. Scaffolds requiring each iteration to cite, instantiate, and adapt a prior method shift agents toward method-guided exploration. The scaffolded agent autonomously composes -- without human design input -- a data-selection policy that outperforms strong published baselines at one-tenth their data budget. Overall, current agents can run the curation loop, but reliable data research requires scaffolded method adaptation, not open-ended prompting alone. Code and benchmark are open-sourced.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Curating training data is among the most consequential yet labor-intensive parts of modern AI development: practitioners iteratively propose, implement, evaluate, and revise data policies against noisy benchmark feedback. We ask whether generalist coding agents can automate this data-curation loop. We introduce CURATION-BENCH, an agent-centric benchmark that fixes the model, training recipe, and evaluation suite while giving agents commandline access to inspect data, implement policies, submit them to fixed training/evaluation pipeline, and revise. However, trajectory analysis reveals persistent execution research gap: agents mainly tune local policy variants rather than explore new policy families, even when given strategy guides and paper references. The scaffolded agent autonomously composes without human design inputa data-selection policy that outperforms strong published baselines at one-tenth their data budget. sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Training data is now first-class design variable in modern AI: it shapes what models can do, how they fail, and how efficiently they learn. Prior work has automated many individual data operations, including pruning (Sorscher et al., 2022; Paul et al., 2021), deduplication (Lee et al., 2022; Abbas et al., 2023; Kang et al., 2025), remixing (Xie et al., 2023; Fan et al., 2024; Chen et al., 2026), rewriting (Wang 1Code and benchmark available at: https://github. com/feiyang-k/curation-bench . 1 Figure 1: Agentic data curation requires more than openended prompting. (a) We formulate data curation as policysearch loop: the agent proposes data policy, constructs training data, observes feedback from fixed training and evaluation, and revises the policy. (b) On 10k-example selection from LLaVA-665K dataset for LLaVA-1.5-7B training, open-ended prompting improves over random selection and human-designed baselines (Wu et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2025), but method-adaptation scaffolding achieves the best result by adapting prior data-selection methods into stronger policy. et al., 2024b; Maini et al., 2024), and synthetic generation (Wang et al., 2023; Taori et al., 2023). What remains largely manual is deciding which technique to use, how to adapt it, and when to revise it after noisy benchmark feedback. Because no technique is universally effective (Zhang et al., 2025; Goyal et al., 2024), practitioners must repeatedly propose, implement, evaluate, and refine data policies. This iterative search is where human effort accumulates. We ask whether the search process itself can be automated. Prior work captures two ingredients needed for automated data curationcontrolled data evaluation and iterative experimentationbut not their combination. Data-centric benchmarks such as DataComp (Gadre et al., 2023; Li et al., 2024) fix the model, training recipe, and evaluation so that data becomes the variable of interest. This isolation principle is essential, but the submitted artifact is static policy, and the benchmark does not evaluate how that policy was discovered, revised, or justified. Autonomous ML-research agents (Yang et al., 2024; Lu et al., 2024; Kon et al., 2026; Karpathy, 2026) evaluate iterative behavior, but typically optimize training recipescode, hyperparameters, architectures, or model dimensionsunder fixed data distribution (Chan et al., 2024; Karpathy, 2026). Data-science agents (Pinchuk, 2026; Jing et al., 2024) iterate over analysis pipelines and predictive models, treating data as input rather than as the object of optimization. What remains missing is an agentic benchmark for iterative data-policy discovery. We introduce CURATION-BENCH to fill this gap. CURATION-BENCH inherits DataComps data-isolation principle by fixing the model, training recipe, and evaluation suite, but replaces static policy submission with an interactive terminal loop: the agent inspects the candidate pool, implements curation policies, submits them to fixed training/evaluation pipeline, observes feedback, and revises. CURATION-BENCH records the full curation trajectorypolicy scripts, data manifests, training outputs, and evaluation logsso that agents can be evaluated not only by final accuracy, but also by how they search: whether they explore new data-policy families, ground decisions in prior evidence, or fall back on shallow local adjustments, and whether those choices improve performance. We study the most immediate candidates for automating iterative data-policy discovery: generalist coding agents, off-the-shelf terminal systems not designed specifically for data curation. They can already inspect files, write scripts, launch jobs, parse logs, and recover from failures. We ask how far such generic execution capabilities can go in data curation, and what additional guidance helps agents make better data-policy decisions. We instantiate CURATION-BENCH primarily in multimodal instruction tuning, where candidate pools mix heterogeneous data sources, evaluations cover diverse visual and linguistic skills, and no single selection heuristic is uniformly effective; we further include smaller-scale CLIP-style pretraining setting to test whether the framework extends beyond instruction tuning alone. The main instantiation yields two findings. First, generalist agents are already useful data-curation executors: under open-ended prompting, they reach strong published data-selection baselines within ten iterations, recovering roughly 60% of the full-data fine-tuning gain at 1.5% of the original pool. Second, trajectory diagnostics reveal persistent execution research gap: agents reliably run the loop but mainly tune local policy variantssource ratios, length thresholds, and random seedsrather than explore distinct policy families. This persists even with strategy guides and paper references, suggesting that the bottleneck is not missing methodological knowledge but failure to operationalize it into executable data policies. We therefore study scaffolds as interventions and find that mandatory citation, instantiation, and adaptation of prior methods shift agents toward method-guided exploration of new policy families. Under the strongest scaffold, the agent composes an EL2N-style top-loss policy (Paul et al., 2021) with an assistant-loss noise filter, producing hybrid loss-based data-selection policy that outperforms the compared published non-agent baselines using one-tenth as much data. Finally, longer-session experiments suggest that agent search iterations are meaningful form of curation compute. When we increase the agent budget beyond ten iterations, average outcomes continue to improve up to 50 iterations without clear plateau. This connects agentic data curation to broader question of how to improve performance in finite-data, increasing-compute regime: when additional raw data is unavailable or increasingly costly, extra compute can be spent not only on reusing data during training or expanding it synthetically (Muennighoff et al., 2023; Kim et al., 2026), but also on searching over how to select, adapt, and validate the data already available.

sections.title: 2 CURATION-BENCH
sections.content: This section specifies the benchmark contract used throughout the paper. CURATION-BENCH task presents an agent with candidate data pool and fixed trainingevaluation pipeline, and asks the agent to produce budgeted training set through an executable data policy. The benchmark constrains what the agent may change, validates each submitted dataset before training, and records the full sequence of policy attempts and outcomes. This contract separates two evaluation targets: the quality of the final curated data and the quality of the agents search process. We first describe the design principles that define this contract, then give the task formalization, environment interface, and scoring metrics. Design principles. The benchmark contract follows four principles. (P1) Data isolation. The model architecture, optimizer, training schedule, and evaluation suite are fixed by the harness. The agents only controlled variable is the curated data. (P2) Terminal realism. The agent operates through standard terminal workspace, mirroring 2 Table 1: Rubric for annotating each agent iteration. Labels are assigned with LLMs (Claude Opus 4.7).

sections.title: 2.2 Scoring and Trajectory Diagnostics
sections.content: Agent sessions are evaluated along two axes: downstream outcome and trajectory quality. Outcome metrics. For session of iterations, the primary outcome is the best score achieved during the session. We report absolute score, improvement over the base model and over random selection at the same budget, and the fraction of the full-data fine-tuning gain recovered (%) when possible. The latter metric provides more straightforward measure of data efficacy. Trajectory diagnostics. Outcome scores do not fully characterize the quality of agentic data curation: high score may come from systematic exploration, but it may also come from repeated local tuning or lucky variation. We therefore annotate each iteration with four labels: new policy family, grounded, effective, and shallow, following the rubrics in Table 1. We report these labels both at the iteration level, for individual inspectcurate trainevaluate loops, and at the trajectory level, for the full multi-iteration session. These diagnostics make the agents search process auditable and show where the search stalls.

sections.title: 3.1 Open-Prompt Agent Setup
sections.content: We evaluate generalist coding agents in the CURATION-BENCH environment under the miniFigure 2: Architecture of CURATION-BENCH. coding agent inspects the candidate pool, implements data policies, and submits curated datasets. The harness validates each submission and scores it with fixed trainingevaluation pipeline. how current generalist coding agents interact with repositories: inspecting files, writing scripts, running commands, debugging failures, and reading logs. (P3) Contamination control. Every submitted dataset must pass an automatic audit before training. The audit checks exact matches and high-overlap text spans against evaluation question answer pairs, preventing direct leakage from becoming trivial path to improvement. (P4) Trajectory legibility. Each iteration persists the curation script, generated manifest, audit result, training output, evaluation scores, and run notes under commit hash. These records support the trajectory diagnostics in Section 2.2.

sections.title: Heuristics
sections.content: Finding 2: Open-prompt agents make weakly grounded choices and over-exploit local policy families. Despite favorable final scores, openprompt agents show two recurring failure modes. First, their stated rationales are often weakly grounded. Run notes typically invoke plausible but generic goalsfilter low-quality examples, increase diversity, try different random seeds, or balance domainswithout tying the next policy to concrete evidence source, hypothesis, or ablation (Tables 3,23). This produces form of vibe optimization: the plan sounds like data research, but the operational criterion is underspecified. it usually instantiates the lowest-cost version of the idea: source-ratio change, response-length threshold, metadata filter, or seed change. These local moves might still improve the score, but they keep the search within the same policy family. Once the agent finds workable family, it rarely pivots to another, and the final outcome is bounded by that family (Tables 3,5).

sections.title: Exploration
sections.content: The previous section shows that open-prompt agents are useful executors but narrow researchers: they can run the loop and improve scores, yet they tend to settle into local data-policy edits. We now introduce scaffold ladder with different levels of guidance to change this behavior. The key question is whether guidance merely changes what agents write in their plans, or whether it changes the policies they actually execute.

sections.title: 4.1 Scaffold Design
sections.content: We study two light scaffolds and two heavy scaffolds. The light scaffolds are designed as awareness-level interventions. Data strategies gives the agent short list of possible policy families, including source balancing, quality filtering, diversity sampling, task targeting, deduplication, difficulty scoring, conversation-structure filtering, and image-metadata filtering. Research papers gives the agent access to paper-derived skill cards and asks it to consult them when useful. These scaffolds expand the agents stated option set, but do not require specific decision protocol. Second, when the agent commits to manifest, The heavy scaffolds instead impose binding re5 Table 4: Scaffold conditions. Light scaffolds provide optional guidance; heavy scaffolds make research discipline part of the task contract.

sections.content: Select at most examples; inspect data, write scripts, submit manifest, read logs, and iterate. Curation strategies include quality filtering, source balancing, semantic diversity, task balancing, deduplication, and length/format filters. List available paper-derived skill cards and consider using them when helpful. Before editing the policy, write an observation, hypothesis, expected effect, and minimal change. The observation must cite concrete evidence. For every non-baseline iteration, choose prior method, explain why it applies, adapt it to the available fields and budget, implement the policy, and validate the manifest. search protocol. In self-research, each non-baseline iteration must begin with written observation, hypothesis, expected metric effect, and minimal policy change, grounded in prior runs or evaluation logs. In adapt papers, each non-baseline iteration must cite specific prior method or skill card, explain why it applies, adapt it to the available fields and budget, and validate the resulting manifest before training. The scaffold constrains the structure of the research loop, but not the policy content: the agent still chooses which failure mode to address, which method to adapt, and how to implement the adaptation. Essentially, light scaffolds test whether making the solution space visible is enough; heavy scaffolds test whether the agent needs procedural constraint that forces evidence-grounded or methodgrounded execution. 4.

sections.title: Intervention Outcome
sections.content: Figure 4 and Table 5 summarize the scaffold results. The main pattern is that awareness-level scaffolds broaden what the agent considers, but protocol-level scaffolds change what the agent executes. Light scaffolds reduce variance and increase some trajectory quality metrics, but they do not materially improve the best outcome over the openprompt condition. The strongest result comes from the paper-adaptation scaffold: its best 10k policy reaches 34.9 average score, exceeding the openprompt agent and the evaluated 100k ARDS baseline while using one-tenth as many examples. Figure 4: Agentic data-curation results with different scaffolds (Claude Code, 10-iteration). Fine-tuning pre-trained LLaVA-1.5-7B on curated subsets of 10k samples from the LLaVA-665K instruction-tuning pool and evaluating on 8 benchmarks. Scaffolding significantly alters agents behavior and outcomes. Compared to open-prompt, light scaffolds reduce outcome variances but do not improve the best outcome. Heavy scaffolds change the outcome substantially, but the effects may be for the better or for the worse depending on the specific scaffold design. quality. The data-strategies scaffold increases the rate of new-policy-family moves from 27% to 43%, suggesting that the agent does consider broader space when policy families are surfaced explicitly. The research-paper scaffold increases grounding from 57% to 70% and reduces shallow moves from 47% to 37%, indicating non-trivial trajectory changes. However, neither light scaffold improves the best outcome beyond the open-prompt maximum of 34.0. The data-strategies condition reaches the same maximum, and the research-paper condition remains below it. The agent names richer policy families, but still tends to instantiate low-cost variants: quota changes, length filters, simple quality proxies, or scaled versions of previously successful mixtures (Table 25,26). This is rational short-horizon behavior. Once the agent finds workable family from prior runs, local edits preserve information from prior runs and have reasonable chance of producing small gains. Light scaffolds make different policy options visible, but do not force the agent to pay the implementation cost. Finding 4: heavy scaffolds enforce grounding and enable larger breakthroughs. The heavy scaffolds change the executed trajectory more substantially. Self-research increases grounded iterations from 57% to 91% and reduces shallow operations from 47% to 9%. Adapt-papers is stronger: all iterations are grounded, shallow operations are eliminated, and the rate of new-policy-family moves rises to 67%. This indicates that the scaffold is not merely changing the wording of the agents notes; it changes what the agent actually tries. The effect is clearest in the best adapt-papers trajectory. After several failed method adaptations, the agent moves into training-dynamics family: it uses an EL2N-style loss proxy to select high-loss examples, then refines that policy with an assistantloss noise filter to remove the highest-loss samples likely to reflect incoherent or noisy responses. This produces the best 10k result, 34.9, above the openprompt agent and the evaluated 100k baselines. This result also explains why trajectory diagnostics are needed. Heavy scaffolds do not necessarily maximize the number of locally effective iterations. The adapt-papers scaffold has only 20% effective iterations by the immediate-improvement criterion, lower than the open-prompt condition. But the trajectory reaches policy family that open-prompt search did not reach. For autonomous data research, the important quantity is therefore not only the rate of small local gains, but whether the search reaches higher-upside regions of the policy space. The scaffold comparison should not be read as showing that heavier is always better. Selfresearch improves grounding but lowers the final score vs. open-prompt. Heavy scaffolds combine several ingredientsmethod grounding, structured logging, category rotation, and procedural constraintsso the experiment does not isolate single causal component. The more conservative conclusion is that awareness alone is insufficient: to convert agent execution into policy exploration, the scaffold must make evidence-grounded or methodgrounded action part of the task contract.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: Data size Avg. score % of full gain 0 665k 10k 10k 10k 10k 10k 10k 10k 10k 10k 50k 100k 50k 100k 28.8 37.1 32.5 32.6 0% 100% 45% 46% 33.4 (+0.8) 34.1 (+1.5) 32.8 (+0.2) 34.0 (+1.4) 34.7 (+2.1) 33.1 (+0.5) 33.6 (+1.0) 33.40.2 33.70.2 33.5 33.8 55% (+9%) 64% (+18%) 48% (+2%) 63% (+17%) 71% (+25%) 52% (+6%) 58% (+12%) 55%3% 59%3% 57% 60% This paper introduced CURATION-BENCH, benchmark for evaluating whether generalist agents can conduct training-data curation as an iterative policy-search process. By fixing the model, training recipe, and evaluation suite, CURATIONBENCH isolates the curated data policy as the object of optimization and records the full trajectory of agent decisions. Our results show both promise and limitation. Open-prompt agents are already useful datathey produce valid datasets, curation executors: run the training/evaluation loop, and match strong human-designed selection baselines at small data budgets. However, trajectory diagnostics reveal an executionresearch gap: agents often improve scores through local heuristic edits rather than by exploring distinct policy families. Scaffold design is the central lever we identify for narrowing this gap. Awareness-level scaffolds broaden plans but do not reliably change executed policies; methodadaptation scaffolds force agents to operationalize prior work and can open higher-upside regions of the data-policy space. These findings suggest that future evaluations of autonomous data research should report not only final scores, but also scaffold strength and trajectory diagnostics: open-ended instructions and papergrounded protocols answer different scientific questions. Figure 5: Ablation on the number of iterations (10-50) in each agent session (Claude Code). result is notable: average performance continues to improve within this range rather than clearly plateauing. Under open-prompting, the gains accumulate gradually, consistent with extended local search over source mixtures and related heuristics. Under the Heavy II scaffold, the best score is already found within the first 10 iterations, but additional iterations reduce variance and move the average upward. This suggests that the agent iteration budget is itself meaningful compute axis for data curation. In finite-data regime, where additional raw data is unavailable, expensive, or lower quality, progress need not come only from training on more examples. Additional compute can instead be spent on searching over data policies: selecting different subsets, testing distinct policy families, adapting prior methods, or rewriting examples. From this perspective, CURATION-BENCH provides way to study how curation quality scales with search compute under fixed data pool.

sections.title: 5.2 Rewriting Extension
sections.content: The selection experiments optimize which examples to train on. To test whether CURATIONBENCH also supports richer data actions, we instantiate it as data-rewriting task: the agent selects examples, rewrites them with an external multimodal large language model (MLLM) tool, verifies the rewritten samples, and submits the transformed dataset through the same validation, contamination, training, and evaluation loop. This setting uses stricter self-research-like scaffold. Every postbaseline iteration must inspect raw evaluation failures, name failure mode, select source examples tied to that mode, rewrite them with the MLLM, verify the rewritten examples, and pass contamination checks before training. Full rewrite scaffold is provided in code repo. Results  (Table 6)  show that CURATION-BENCH is not limited to subset selec-

sections.title: References
sections.content: Our experiments instantiate CURATION-BENCH primarily in vision-language instruction tuning, with one smaller CLIP-style pretraining instantiation and rewriting extension. The conclusions may differ for pretraining mixtures, code, math, domain-specific instruction tuning, or settings where evaluation is less reliable. Our scaffold comparison is also not full factorial study: heavy scaffolds combine method grounding, structured logging, and stronger procedural constraints, so we identify useful design pattern rather than isolating single causal ingredient. Finally, trajectory labels require annotation; although they make agent behavior more legible, they introduce their own judgment calls and should be reported with rubrics and examples.

sections.title: Broader Impacts
sections.content: Agentic data curation could reduce the cost of finetuning by identifying smaller, higher-value subsets, but it also increases the need for oversight. Agentcurated datasets may amplify biases, remove underrepresented examples, or optimize toward narrow evaluation targets if deployed without review. Because paper-grounded scaffolds lower the engineering barrier for data optimization, they could also accelerate harmful applications. We therefore recommend treating agent-curated datasets as candidate artifacts subject to human or programmatic review, reporting scaffold strength and trajectory diagnostics alongside outcome scores, and retaining contamination checks and held-out evaluations.

sections.title: A Extended Related Work
sections.content: Autonomous research and machine-learning agents. growing line of work evaluates languagemodel agents in interactive environments where they read files, edit code, run commands, and observe feedback. AgentBench introduced broad multi-environment benchmark for LLM agents (Liu et al., 2023b); MLAgentBench and MLE-bench evaluate machine-learning experimentation and Kaggle-style engineering (Huang et al., 2023; Chan et al., 2024); PostTrainBench evaluates whether CLI agents can post-train base LLMs under bounded compute (Rank et al., 2026); and Terminal-Bench broadens the setting to general command-line tasks (Merrill et al., 2026). AutoResearch and AI-Scientist-style systems push further, placing agents in iterative loops of idea generation, code modification, experiment execution, and reporting (Karpathy, 2026; Lu et al., 2024; Yamada et al., 2025). Conceptually, the AutoResearch framework extends to optimizing training data in the same way as for model and training configurations; preliminary discussion appears in Karpathy (2026), but systematic study on the data front is absent. CURATION-BENCH fills that gap by isolating training-data curation as the central object of study. Data-centric AI and data selection. Data-centric AI emphasizes dataset quality, coverage, and labeling as first-class levers for model improvement (Ng, 2021). The literature spans data pruning, coreset selection, influence functions, data valuation, quality filtering, and training-dynamics metrics, etc. (Koh and Liang, 2017; Ghorbani and Zou, 2019; Paul et al., 2021; Sorscher et al., 2022; Northcutt et al., 2021). EL2N and GraNd identify important examples early in training (Paul et al., 2021); instruction-tuning data selection has been studied via quality scoring, diversity, influence approximation, and targeted selection (Xia et al., 2024); and Self-Instruct-style pipelines show that LLMs can also generate instruction data that requires its own filtering and auditing (Wang et al., 2023). Our work is motivated by this literature but targets different question: useful data agent must not just execute known methodit must decide which method family is appropriate, implement it under budget, inspect evidence, and revise the policy. Data-science agents. Data-science benchmarks evaluate agents that analyze datasets, write code, produce notebooks, and build tabular models. DSEval covers the data-science lifecycle (Zhang et al., 2024); DSBench draws realistic analysis and modeling tasks from sources such as Kaggle and ModelOff (Jing et al., 2024); and TML-Bench evaluates autonomous agents on tabular ML tasks under time limits (Pinchuk, 2026). These targets are valuable but are usually analysis or predictive modeling; the artifact being optimized is model or report, not training corpus. CURATION-BENCH instead measures whether agents can produce data policy whose value is measured by downstream model behavior on generativemodel training. Agent benchmarks. Agent benchmarks such as AgentBench, MLAgentBench, MLE-bench, PostTrainBench, Terminal-Bench, AutoResearch, and AI-Scientist-style systems evaluate whether agents can operate in interactive environments, modify code, run experiments, and report results (Liu et al., 2023b; Huang et al., 2023; Chan et al., 2024; Rank et al., 2026; Merrill et al., 2026; Karpathy, 2026; Lu et al., 2024; Yamada et al., 2025). Data-centric AI and data-selection research provide many non-agentic methods for pruning, scoring, filtering, and valuing examples (Ng, 2021; Koh and Liang, 2017; Ghorbani and Zou, 2019; Paul et al., 2021; Sorscher et al., 2022; Northcutt et al., 2021; Xia et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2023). Data-science-agent benchmarks evaluate analysis, notebook construction, and tabular modeling rather than the downstream value of curated training corpus (Zhang et al., 2024; Jing et al., 2024; Pinchuk, 2026). CURATION-BENCH fills the gap summarized in Table 7: it evaluates whether generalist agents can discover, implement, and revise data policies whose value is measured by downstream model behavior. Details for CURATION-BENCH B.1 Source data and models. The benchmark draws training pools from two public visual-instruction mixtures and fine-tunes one of four base VLMs. 14 Table 7: Positioning relative to agent and data-centric benchmarks. The table intentionally focuses on whether the benchmark makes training-data curation the central research object.

sections.content: Optimizes pipeline under fixed data distribution; CURATION-BENCH isolates the data distribution itself as the object of optimization. Data side is usually one of many levers, not isolated as the benchmark target. Evaluates broad ML experimentation rather than data policy discovery. Data preparation matters, but objective is competition performance, not data curation mechanisms. Evaluates end-to-end post-training; our focus is trace-level diagnosis and scaffolds for data strategy exploration. Data is an input to analysis/modeling rather than the artifact being optimized. Provides methods, but not an agent benchmark for autonomous data research. CURATION-BENCH (this work)

sections.title: Yes
sections.content: Measures whether generalist agents can discover and execute data policies. LLaVA-665K. Following Liu et al. (2024a), we use the llava_v1_5_mix665k.json mixture, materialized locally as single Arrow dataset of 665,298 rows with three columns (images, texts, source_subset). Rows are tagged by their originating source_subset, partitioning the pool into six groups: coco (364,100), vg (86,417), ocr_vqa (80,000), gqa (72,140), text_only (40,688, image-free ShareGPT turns), and textvqa (21,953). Vision-Flan. We additionally use the human-annotated Vision-Flan (Xu et al., 2024) mixture, materialized as single Arrow dataset with three columns (images, texts, source_subset). It has over 191 task types (approximately 1,000 samples per task, following the released annotation_191-task_1k layout). Models. Each task pins single model_key so that fine-tuning always starts from the same pre-curation checkpoint: LLaVA-1.5-7B (Liu et al., 2024a) visual instruction-tuning stage; we full-finetune the language model with the vision tower frozen. Checkpoint released with the artifact as llava-1.5-7b-init. Qwen2-VL-2B (base) (Wang et al., 2024a) visual instruction-tuning stage; we full-finetune the entire model (vision + encoder + projector merger + language model). Checkpoint can be found in Qwen2-VL-2B. Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct (Bai et al., 2025) post-training stage starting from the released instruction-tuned checkpoint (Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct). We full-finetune the entire model (visual encoder + projector merger + language model), with no module frozen. SmolVLM-2.2B-Base (Marafioti et al., 2025) visual instruction-tuning stage starting from the pre-instruct base checkpoint; we full-finetune the language model and the projector with the SigLIP vision tower frozen. Checkpoint can be found in SmolVLM-Base. DataComp-Small. For the CLIP pretraining instantiation we use the Small configuration of DataComp (Gadre et al., 2023), the 12.8M-pair filtering-track pool. After downloading we recovered 8.77M of the 12.8M pairs (68.5% of the original pool, the remainder lost to link rot) and resized all images to 256 before storage. The filtering-track rules fix the candidate pool, training compute budget, model 15 architecture, and evaluation suite; the agents only degree of freedom is the subset of pairs retained for training. B.2 Contamination audit Before fine-tuning, an agent may run contamination audit on its candidate submission to check for overlap with the eight target benchmarks of CURATION-BENCH (HallusionBench, LLaVABench, MMBench, MMMU_DEV_VAL, MMStar, MMVet, MathVista_MINI, OCRBench). We build normalized question answer string for each conversation turn in the submission and for each evaluation Q/A pair (lowercase, punctuation stripped, whitespace collapsed), and check (i) exact matches via SHA-256 hashing and (ii) near-duplicates via word 8-gram overlap with similarity 0.8. The audit aggregates per-benchmark exact-match rate re and near-duplicate rate rn and returns HIGH_RISK (re 0.10 or rn 0.20), WARNING (re 0.05 or rn 0.10), or CLEAN otherwise. HIGH_RISK status forces the agent to revise its selection and re-audit before submission can proceed; WARNING does not block submission but reports the overlap to alert the agent; CLEAN proceeds normally. B.3 Submission validation Submission is the gate that pins candidate dataset as the input to the training stage. We enforce two conditions: (i) schema: the path is loadable as HuggingFace dataset with columns images and texts; (ii) budget: the row count equals the tasks target_rows; validated submission is archived into the run directory and any prior fine-tuning result for the iteration is invalidated, so submission acts as commit point: the agent may iterate freely before calling submit, but once accepted the dataset is frozen for the rest of the iteration. B.4 Training CURATION-BENCH fixes the entire fine-tuning stage so that the only variable across an agents iterations is the curated subset. Given submitted subset, the harness fine-tunes the tasks target model on single NVIDIA A100 GPU for one epoch under fixed recipe; no training hyperparameter is exposed to the agent. Each target model in the suite has its own recipe. For LLaVA-1.5-7B and SmolVLM-Base, the vision tower is frozen and only the language model and the multi-modal projector are updated; the detailed training configurations can be found in Table 8 (LLaVA-1.5-7B, except for the Training hardware) and Table 11 (SmolVLM-Base). For Qwen2-VL-2B and Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct, the entire model is full-finetuned with FlashAttention-2 (Tables 9 and 10). Table 8: Fixed fine-tuning recipe used for all agent and baseline submissions.

The protocol VLMEvalKit wrapper, the eight target benchmarks, the shared Qwen3.5-27B judge endpoint  (Table 16)  , and the aggregation rule  (Table 17)  is exactly the one described in Section D.3. For the DataComp instantiation, the harness uses the standard DataComp evaluation suite (Gadre et al., 2023): 38 zero-shot tasks spanning ImageNet and its distribution shifts, VTAB classification tasks, retrieval (Flickr30k, MSCOCO), and additional classification benchmarks. We report the unweighted mean accuracy across the 38 tasks, matching the filtering-track leaderboard. Evaluation takes 2 hours per run on single H100. Scaffolds (open-prompt, light, heavy) C.1 Common Failures for Open-Prompt Agents Table 13 summarizes common failure modes of open-prompt generalist agents in data curation tasks. Table 14 provides non-exhaustive taxonomy of data-curation strategy families. The families are partially disconnected: moving from one to another often requires new tools, proxies, and assumptions. C.2 Light Scaffolds The design principle of our light scaffolds is to remain task-agnostic so that the same prompt could plausibly ship as default for any data-curation task. The goal of this paper is not to add another data-curation strategy but to make ready-to-use generalist agents practical for frontline practitioners. We explore two strategies that best embody that ideology: (I) providing list of curation strategies organized by policy family and instructing the agent to explore them, and (II) compiling research papers on data tasks into skill cards for the agent to reference. I. Concise instructions toward desired trajectories. To counter the agents tendency to fixate on single strategy family, we surface list of diverse operations upfront and prompt the agent to explore beyond them. The instruction is deliberately framed at the level of policy families rather than task-specific Table 13: Common failure modes of open-prompt generalist agents on data curation tasks.

sections.title: Selection
sections.content: Agent creates examples too close to evaluation outputs. Detailed pipelines improve scores but reduce autonomy. Agent is told to rewrite but only selects existing examples. or novelty policy-family Require evidence-backed ablations. Data audit reports, quality classifiers, and hypothesis templates. Fixed seed protocols and paired comparisons. Skill cards with executable entry points and mandatory decision checkpoints. Force comparison across policy families before local search. Structured error extraction, clustering, and traceable rewrite plans. Contamination checks, hidden evals, and red-team audits. Report scaffold strength as an experimental variable. Separate tools and budgets for rewrite generation and validation. Table 14: non-exhaustive taxonomy of data-curation strategy families. The families are partially disconnected: moving from one to another often requires new tools, proxies, and assumptions (Bhardwaj et al., 2024; Maghoumi and Liu, 2024; NVIDIA, 2024).

sections.title: Quality and format
sections.content: Distribution/domain Semantic coverage Task/instruction level Training dynamics Dedup/contamination Style and response form (rewriting) Generation feedback (data synthesis) Remove malformed, low-quality, or unsafe examples Tune source and domain mixture ratios Cluster examples, maximize diversity, cover skills Balance task types, tools, reasoning modes Select by loss, EL2N, gradient norm, forgetting Remove near-duplicates and benchmark leakage Rewrite for concise, tool-using, or stepwise style Add data from model failures or self-play Validators, quality models, metadata Domain labels, mixture search Embeddings, clustering, facility-location objectives Task classifiers, taxonomies Warm-up training, per-example statistics MinHash, embedding similarity, benchmark filters Rewriting model, validators Eval logs, error classifiers, synthetic generation recipes: this expands the agents awareness of the solution space without prescribing implementations and preserves autonomy over how each idea is instantiated. II. Research papers as skill cards. The value of human data experts lies in knowing which methods are viable for given task and when each is most likely to help. Rather than hand-injecting that expertise per task, we compile relevant research papers into reusable skill cards that the agent can consult on its own. Concretely, we prompt Claude Code with an automated pipeline (Appendix G) to compile library of skill cards from VLM data-curation papers, each formatted as decision-first cookbook entry rather than paper summary. The compilation is offlineskill cards are prepared before any agent runand the agent prompt is augmented with two short notes: list all available skill cards at the start of each session, and use the reference skills when helpful. Skill cards compiled from research papers on data curation vision-language models (VLMs) ## Characteristics of the skills Four top-level buckets, each stage of the VLM data pipeline: data-acquisition, data-curation, data-selection, data-synthesis Each skill is one folder containing exactly one file: SKILL.md with title that combines the papers short title and source. 1 paper -> 1 skill. No multi-paper aggregations; no extra assets (no code, no figures, no PDFs). **Per-file structure (rigidly templated)** **Tone / philosophy** Decision-first ("Use this skill when. . . " / "Avoid it when. . . ") rather than paper summary. Procedures are skeletons the *shape* of the method, not full reproduction. Each step ends with "See paper for details." Knowledge-cards optimized for downstream agent to retrieve and act on, not for human reading. They look like cookbook recipe cards keyed by paper. Common Failures under Light Scaffolding. Table 15 summarizes the failures: light interventions improve the written plan but rarely the executed trajectory. The agent acknowledges the broader options and then chooses the easiest implementable heuristic. The bottleneck is not knowledgethe agent often names training dynamics, semantic clustering, or rewritingbut the conversion of high-level idea into valid, budget-aware implementation with the right diagnostics. This is the recurrent pattern: current generalist agents are strong executors but weak autonomous researchers, capable of running the benchmark, writing scripts, debugging failures, and improving local policy, yet unable to operationalize abstract data-research ideas without additional structure. Table 15: Interventions with light-scaffolding and observed failure modes.

sections.title: Ask to avoid heuristics
sections.content: Let the agent autonomously improve data. Encourage movement across policy families. Suppress trivial length/score filters.

sections.title: Provide flat skill list
sections.content: Expose many approaches.

sections.title: Ask to inspect raw eval logs
sections.content: Encourage targeted rewriting. Defaults to filtering, deduplication, simple score thresholds. Plans mention many families; implementation remains local. Agent renames heuristics as quality criteria or uses them indirectly. Skills are rarely used or used superficially without correct evidence. Agent reads summary metrics or selects examples, but rarely rewrites with grounded error taxonomy. C.3 Heavy Scaffolds I. Self-Research reshapes the workflow into structured research loop where every experiment must be motivated by concrete observation, documented with hypothesis, and logged with its findingspositive or negativeto inform the next iteration. Examples of agent-written research plans are provided in the code repo. 20 Heavy-scaffolding I: Self-research Research-first mandate requiring that every iteration (beyond baselines) begin with actual data research rather than intuitive code changes. Before implementing the curation script, the agent is instructed to first write research.md for the iteration containing an observation, hypothesis, and minimal change all grounded in concrete data findings, not generic intuition. Blocklist of exhausted strategies: The research version adds an explicit list of patterns that are "out" strategies based on trivial operations and shallow ideas. Richer result logging schema: The agent is instructed to document outcomes at each iteration with an explicit template including three additional fields"skill ref" (which prior research.md commit this iteration builds on), "failure mode" (what new finding the run revealed, tied to specific benchmarks), and "next skill candidate" (the next research question to investigate). This creates chain of reasoning across iterations to discourage ungrounded moves without reasonable rationale or logic. II. Adapt Research Papers hardens the soft prioritize skills guidance from light-scaffolding II into strict protocol: every iteration must cite specific skill, adapt concrete procedure from it, rotate across skill categories, and avoid defined set of low-effort heuristics. The result is more disciplined and auditable research loop. Heavy-scaffolding II: Adapt Research Papers Session initialization: listing skills -> deeply reading skills: at the start of the session, the agent must browse all skill categories, pick at least 3 skills per category that seem relevant, and read those skill cards in full before writing its curation script. The agent is also prompted to use Agent/Explore tools to scan quickly through the one-line decision notes in the skill cards. The pipeline raises the bar from awareness to comprehension. Strategy must be skill-grounded: formalizing the requirement that the curation script at each iteration must be grounded in specific skill card. The workflow is: identify failure mode from the last result, pick 13 relevant skills, read them fully, then state in the logs specific skill card and one-line adaptation. This changes implicit/soft guidance in the light-scaffolding ("prioritize exploration with reference skills") to hard rule. Category rotation requirement: the heavy-scaffolding requires that before repeating skill category, the agent must attempt at least one iteration from each of the other three. The next skill candidate must also come from category not used in the last three iterations unless justified. Blocklist of exhausted strategies and Richer result logging schema are enforced similarly to Heavy-scaffolding I. The agent is forbidden from citing skill as mere "inspiration" rather than an adapted procedure. Not citing specific skill card in non-baseline row is explicitly called "rule violation." C.4 Rewriting Scaffolds Scaffolding for rewriting. The rewriting scaffold introduces two capabilities absent from the selectiononly scaffolds. First, eval-log reading: after each evaluation, the agent samples 515 incorrect predictions per benchmark, categorizes the failure mode (e.g., OCR misread, format violation, hallucinated option, missing reasoning step), and cross-references the training data to identify which examplesor gaps produced the failure. Second, targeted rewriting: the agent constructs rewrite recipe specifying (a) which slice of the training data to target, (b) what transformation to apply via the MLLM, and (c) the expected effect on the identified failure mode. The recipe is logged alongside the iteration result, creating an auditable chain from failure observation to rewrite action to training outcome. Full scaffold details are in Appendix G. We adopt the self-research heavy scaffold for rewriting. Each iteration must begin with evidence from evaluation logs or data inspection, name concrete failure mode, select source examples tied to that failure, rewrite them with an external MLLM tool, and validate the rewritten outputs before training. The scaffold therefore changes the task from pick the best examples to pick and improve examples under fixed training budget. Compared with template rewriting, the setup is more agentic: the rewrite policy is not fixed in advance, and the agent must decide which examples to rewrite, what transformation to apply, and how aggressively to use the rewriting model.

sections.title: D Experimental Details and Additional Results
sections.content: D.1 Terminal Agents All agents interact with the CURATION-BENCH environment through terminal interface: they read files, write and edit scripts, run shell commands, and observe stdout/stderr. The agents differ in their backbone model, harness, and hosting, but share the same workspace, CLI, and submission gate. Claude Code. We use the Claude Code terminal agent (Anthropic, 2025) with two backbone models: Opus 4.7 (primary) and Sonnet 4.6 (ablation). Claude Code provides built-in tool use for file editing, command execution, and multi-step planning. No additional plugins or custom tools are added beyond the benchmark CLI. Codex. We use OpenAIs Codex terminal agent with two backbone models: GPT-5.4 (primary) and GPT-5.3 (ablation). Codex operates in sandboxed environment with the same file-system layout and CLI access as the Claude Code runs. Open-source models via OpenHands. To evaluate open-source agent backbones, we use the OpenHands harness (Wang et al., 2025), which wraps an LLM in terminal-agent loop with file editing, shell execution, and observation parsing. We evaluate two models: Kimi K2.5 and Qwen3.5-397B. Both are served via the Together AI API2 using an OpenAI-compatible chat-completion endpoint, which OpenHands consumes as its backend LLM. Inference parameters (temperature, max tokens) follow the OpenHands defaults for each model. Shared configuration. Across all agents, the Docker workspace, candidate-pool access, benchmark CLI, submission validation, contamination audit, training recipe, and evaluation suite are identical. The only differences are the agent backbone and harness; no agent receives task-specific tools or data-curation expertise beyond what the scaffold condition specifies  (Table 4)  . D.2 DataComp Filtering Setup For the CLIP pretraining instantiation, all agent and baseline submissions use the DataComp-Small filtering-track configuration described in Section B: the same 12.8M-pair candidate pool (of which 8.77M pairs were recoverable after link rot, see Section B), the same OpenCLIP ViT-B/32 training recipe  (Table 12)  , and the same 38-task zero-shot evaluation suite (Gadre et al., 2023). This subsection records only the differences in what was run rather than how it was run. Agent We evaluate Claude Code (Opus 4.7) on this instantiation. The agent operates over the metadata fields available in the DataComp pool, implements filtering policy, submits the filtered subset through the harness CLI, and revises after observing the DataComp-suite score. Baseline We compare against the published top-30% CLIP score DataComp filtering baseline (Gadre et al., 2023): pairs are kept when their similarity score under frozen CLIP ViT-L/14 reference encoder is in the top 30% of the pool, after which ViT-B/32 is trained from scratch under the same Small-track recipe. Resources per session single training-and-evaluation cycle for one filtered subset takes 4 hours of H100 time (2 training, 2 evaluation, Table 12). D.3 Shared Training and Evaluation Protocol In experiments with fine-tuning pre-trained LLaVA-1.5-7B models on examples from the LLaVA-665K dataset, all submitted datasets are trained and evaluated through the same wrapper. Each submitted subset is fine-tuned for one epoch with the fixed recipe in Table 8 and evaluated by the same eight-benchmark VLMEvalKit pipeline (Duan et al., 2024). 2https://www.together.ai/ 22 Source data and model. All subsets are drawn from the LLaVA-665K mixture (Liu et al., 2024a) released on Hugging Face at https://huggingface.co/datasets/liuhaotian/ LLaVA-Instruct-150K/blob/main/llava_v1_5_mix665k.json, materialized locally as five Arrow splits: coco (364,100), gqa (72,140), ocr_vqa (80,000), textvqa (21,953), vg (86,417), and text_only (40,688). Training always starts from the same pre-data-curation LLaVA-1.5-7B checkpoint (Liu et al., 2024a), released with the artifact as HF checkpoint. Training. The per-model procedure is the same across baseline families, budgets, and agent submissions. Each submitted dataset is fine-tuned on one NVIDIA A100 GPU from the same LLaVA-1.5-7B initialization for one epoch with the vision tower frozen. Training uses PyTorch (Paszke et al., 2019) with Accelerate support (Gugger et al., 2022). The trainer uses the same LLaVA chat-template normalization and assistant-token masking for all subsets. Table 8 summarizes the fixed recipe. Evaluation. Each trained checkpoint is evaluated through the repositorys VLMEvalKit wrapper rather than by manually invoking benchmark scripts. The wrapper isolates each benchmarks artifacts, archives stale outputs before reruns, and refuses eval-only mode if the expected trained checkpoint is missing. All checkpoints are evaluated on the same eight VLMEvalKit benchmarks: MMVet (Yu et al., 2023), LLaVA-Bench (Liu et al., 2023a), MMBench (Liu et al., 2024b), MMMU validation (Yue et al., 2024), MMStar (Chen et al., 2024), MathVista-Mini (Lu et al., 2023), OCRBench (Liu et al., 2024c), and HallusionBench (Guan et al., 2024). Candidate-model inference is run by VLMEvalKit (Duan et al., 2024) with vLLM (Kwon et al., 2023). Judge-based metrics use separate OpenAI-compatible Qwen3.527B endpoint (Team, 2026) for all baseline runs. The server-side judge configuration is summarized in Table 16. Table 16: Judge-server configuration for judge-based VLMEvalKit metrics. This endpoint is separate from candidate-model evaluation inference.

For each baseline family and subset size, we generate up to six independently seeded runs. Different runs use different seeds. We report the mean and sample standard deviation across available run-level scores. If method-size cell has fewer than six completed runs, we report the available count rather than treating the cell as complete. D.5 Additional Results and Ablation Studies We run four additional ablations along the axes of CURATION-BENCHs task specification and the agent setup. Each ablation primarily varies one axis while holding the rest fixed at the original open-prompt setting (LLaVA-665K LLaVA-1.5-7B, = 10k, default scaffold, Claude Code Opus 4.7 backbone). The budget and scaffold ablations additionally replicate across both Claude and Codex backbones to test whether their effects hold across model families. Selection budget. We vary {10k, 20k, 50k} on fine-tuning pre-trained LLaVA-1.5-7B models on examples from the LLaVA-665K dataset curated by Claude Code (Opus 4.7)/Codex (GPT-5.4). This tests whether the agents gain over random subset scales with budget. Task generalization. We vary M0 and Dpool at = 10k under Claude Code Opus 4.7: two alternative target modelsQwen2-VL-2B (base-model fine-tuning) and Qwen2.5-VL-3B (post-training)and one alternative data pool, Vision-Flan. This tests whether the curation strategies the agent develops transfer across model scales, training regimes, and pool structures, or are tuned to LLaVA-665Ks specific subset organization. Agent backbone. We compare four agent backbones at = 10k under the default scaffold: Claude Code with Opus 4.7, Claude Code with Sonnet 4.6, Codex with GPT-5.4, and Codex with GPT-5.3. This tests how different agent backbones perform on the same task and scaffold. Scaffold. We compare three scaffolds at = 10k under both Claude and Codex: open-prompt (minimal-scaffolded), Heavy Scaffold (self-research), and Heavy Scaffold II (adapt papers). Table 19 shows results for interventions with heavy-scaffolding (Codex). sections.title: E Extended Discussion
sections.content: E.1 From autonomous execution to agent-assisted research Our results support sharp distinction between two modes of agent progress. In autonomous execution, the human supplies relatively concrete objective and the agent performs steps with clear local verification (fixing code, running training, producing valid submission). In agent-assisted research, the objective is underspecified, the policy space is discontinuous, and evaluation is sparse and noisy. Training-data curation belongs to the second category: there is no oracle that tells the agent whether data strategy is scientifically meaningful before an expensive training run. This distinction reframes the research agenda. Execution tasks may continue to benefit primarily from stronger models, better tools, and longer contexts. Data research additionally requires better scaffolds representations of the data-policy space, mechanisms for grounding hypotheses, protocols for comparing policies under noise, and interfaces that turn prior research into executable agent actions. E.2 Scaffolds as research artifacts scaffold is not merely prompt; it is an experimental-design object that determines what kinds of reasoning and action the agent can perform. In our experiments, weak scaffold produces plans while strong scaffold produces valid data policiesso the same backbone can look like different system depending on its scaffold. Future papers should therefore report scaffold details with the same rigor as model details: score obtained under detailed pipeline with examples is not directly comparable to score obtained under an open-ended instruction. E.3 The autonomyperformance trade-off Heavy scaffolds can improve performance while reducing autonomy, and this is feature to measure rather than flaw to avoid. fully open task asks whether the agent can discover the method; heavily scaffolded task asks whether it can execute and adapt the method. Both are useful, but they answer different questions, and the field will be better served by reporting scaffold-strength ladderopen instruction, broad prompt, skill list, executable skill card, scripted pipelinerather than single number.

sections.title: F Broader Impacts and Considerations
sections.content: F.1 Broader Societal Impacts CURATION-BENCH and the accompanying findings sit at the intersection of two trajectories with mixed societal implications: the increasing capability of generalist coding agents and the increasing centrality of training-data curation to model behavior. We discuss the most direct positive and negative impacts we anticipate. Potential positive impacts. First, automating training-data curation can substantially reduce the human labor and compute required to build effective models. Our strongest observed 10k subset recovers up to 71% of the full-data fine-tuning gain on 665k examples, suggesting that agent-curated subsets can lower the barrier to fine-tuning for practitioners with limited compute, including academic laboratories, public-sector users, and groups in low-resource settings who cannot afford full-pool training. Second, the framework makes data decisions more legible: trajectory diagnostics record why given dataset was selected, which supports auditability of model training pipelines and is useful for downstream accountability. Third, by open-sourcing CURATION-BENCH and the scaffold ladder, we aim to make agent-driven data research shared public artifact rather than proprietary capability concentrated in few labs. Potential negative impacts. The same capabilities admit several concerning failure modes. (i) Reduced human oversight of training data. If agent-curated subsets are deployed without review, harmful biases, unsafe content, or underrepresented groups in the candidate pool may be silently amplified or removed by opaque scoring functions. (ii) Acceleration of capability development for misuse. general recipe for data optimization can in principle be applied to data pools whose downstream models are designed for 31 harmful tasks (e.g., disinformation, targeted persuasion, surveillance-related vision tasks). The scaffolds we studyparticularly the paper-adaptation scaffold that turns published methods into executable agent actionsreduce the engineering friction for any party attempting such optimization, not only beneficial ones. (iii) Concentration of advantage. The strongest results we report require capable backbone agents and non-trivial compute for repeated training/evaluation. If only well-resourced actors can run the heavyscaffold loop effectively, agentic data curation could widen rather than narrow the gap between large industrial labs and the broader community. F.2 Considerations and Safeguards Mitigations we recommend. We encourage three concrete practices when building on this work. First, treat agent-curated datasets as candidate artifacts subject to human or programmatic review for representation, safety, and license compliance, rather than as final training inputs. Second, report scaffold strength and trajectory-level diagnostics alongside outcome scores, so that improvements can be attributed to the agents actual research process rather than to opaque tuning. Third, retain contamination checks and held-out evaluations when operating agents that can read evaluation logs; CURATION-BENCH ships such checks and we recommend they remain enabled in derivative pipelines. F.3 Released Artifacts and Residual Risk The artifacts we release are benchmark harness, scaffolds (prompt specifications), trace logs, and curated dataset manifests (i.e., indices into public datasets). We do not release new pretrained models or new raw multimodal datasets. We have nonetheless taken several precautions. Source data. All candidate pools are existing public datasetsLLaVA-665K (Liu et al., 2024a) and Vision-Flan (Xu et al., 2024)used under their original licenses. Manifests select subsets of these pools rather than redistributing images or text, which preserves the upstream license posture and avoids creating new venue for problematic content. Traceability and legibility. Every iterations curation script, manifest, audit result, training output, evaluation scores, and run notes are persisted under commit hash (P4 in Section 2). This supports post-hoc inspection of agent behavior and makes it possible to identify, retract, or correct individual iterations if downstream issues are discovered. Scope of the released artifact. We judge the misuse risk of releasing CURATION-BENCH itself to be modest: it does not provide novel offensive capabilities, it operates on already-public datasets, and its primary contribution is methodologicalan evaluation framework and scaffolds for studying agent behavior. We will release the benchmark with documentation that explicitly describes the contamination audit, the assumption that downstream users will retain it, and the recommendation that agent-curated datasets be reviewed before deployment. F.4 Declaration of LLM Use LLMs are central components of this work and are declared accordingly. LLMs as the object of study. The agents we evaluateClaude Code with Opus 4.7 and Sonnet 4.6, and Codex with GPT-5.4 and GPT-5.3are themselves LLM-based systems, and they are the experimental subject rather than auxiliary tools. All claims about agent behavior, exploration, and scaffold sensitivity are claims about these systems running under the protocols described in Sections 3,4. LLMs as tools inside the pipeline. Three additional LLM-driven components are part of the methodology rather than the writing process. (i) Trace annotation. The four trajectories labels in Table 1 (new policy, grounded, effective, shallow) are produced with LLM assistance using Claude Opus 4.7, following the rubric in Section 2.2. We treat these labels as diagnostic annotations rather than ground-truth scientific claims, and we provide rubrics and trace examples to support auditing. (ii) Skill-card compilation. The paper-derived skill library used by light scaffold II and heavy scaffold II is generated by an automated Claude Code pipeline against corpus of data-curation papers, following the template in Appendix G. (iii) Rewriting tool and judge. The rewriting experiments call Qwen3.5-2B/9B and Gemma3-4B as rewriting backbones, and judge-based VLMEvalKit metrics use separate Qwen3.5-27B endpoint  (Table 16)  . All of these are documented with prompts, model versions, and configurations sufficient for reproduction. LLMs for writing assistance. Beyond the components above, LLMs were used only in standard writing-support roles (grammar, phrasing, and LaTeX formatting suggestions). All scientific claims, experimental designs, analyses, and conclusions are the authors. This use of LLMs does not affect the methodology, results, or originality of the work, and per the LLM policy is reported here for transparency rather than as methodological dependency. Prompts and Skill-Card Templates Additional full prompts and example skill cards are provided in the code repository. Self-Research Iteration: Shift 200 OCR_VQA to COCO # Research: Shift 200 OCR_VQA to COCO (not TextVQA) ## Observation Three prior experiments changed training data composition. All crashed MMMU: 933dce7 (ocr_vqa 400, textvqa +400): MMMU 34.7 26.0 5da628e (GQA diversity filter): MMMU 34.7 26.0 0dc4697 (COCO short-bias): MMMU 34.7 34.1 (minor) The 933dce7 crash was attributed to OCR_VQA reduction, but it also doubled TextVQA. TextVQA is 100% captioning format (Provide one-sentence caption), which is radically different from MMMUs MC format. The MMMU crash may have been from TextVQAs captioning format conflicting with MC-format capability, not from OCR_VQA reduction. Supporting evidence: 0bf0abb increased OCR weight (oversampled OCR_VQA) and MMMU was 34.8 essentially unchanged. This suggests OCR_VQA count changes dont affect MMMU much, and 933dce7s crash was from TextVQA doubling. OCR_VQA content: 39% yes/no, 21% Who wrote this book?, 21% What is the title? highly formulaic book metadata extraction. Of the 4 retained subsets, OCR_VQA is the most formulaic and least diverse. ## Hypothesis Shifting 200 samples from OCR_VQA to COCO (NOT TextVQA) will: 1. Keep MMMU stable (no TextVQA change, OCR_VQA change is small) 2. Improve visual diversity (200 more unique COCO images) 3. Potentially improve MMVet/LLaVABench (+0.30.5 from more detailed COCO content) 4. Minor OCRBench impact (3 to 5 from slightly less OCR training) Expected net: +0.001 to +0.003 accuracy ## Minimal Change After computing proportional allocations: ocr_vqa: 1486 1286 (200) coco: 6765 6965 (+200) gqa: 1340 (unchanged) textvqa: 408 (unchanged)

sections.title: Instructions for automated compilation of skill cards compiled from research papers
sections.content: ## Characteristics of the skills Four top-level buckets, each stage of the VLM data pipeline: data-acquisition, data-curation, data-selection, data-synthesis Each skill is one folder containing exactly one file: SKILL.md with title that combines the papers short title and source. 1 paper -> 1 skill. No multi-paper aggregations; no extra assets (no code, no figures, no PDFs). **Per-file structure (rigidly templated)** 33 Every SKILL.md uses the same H2 sections in the same order: 1. # {Paper title} 2. **One-line decision** when to use / when not to (single sentence each). 3. **Skill metadata** skill type, paper kind (e.g. operational-method), actionability (high/med/low), evidence quality (full_paper). 4. **Goal** one paragraph operational restatement. 5. **Problem signature** modality, data state, scale regime, model requirement. 6. **Use when** / **Do not use when** bullet pairs. 7. **Required inputs** / **Optional inputs** / **Outputs** bolded named slots with one-line descriptions. 8. **Assumptions and prerequisites**. 9. **Procedure** numbered steps, each with Action: . . . / Why: . . . / Note: See paper for details. 10. **Parameters to set** Role / How to set / Default-or-range / Effect. 11. **Validation checks**, **Failure modes**. 12. **Adaptation notes for VLM training** always present, ties the paper back to VLM data work even when the source paper is not VLM-specific. 13. **Implementation notes**. 14. **Evidence from the paper** 35 bullets with the load-bearing claims. 15. **Source paper** title, year, venue, paper ID, URL, arXiv ID. **Tone / philosophy** Decision-first ("Use this skill when. . . " / "Avoid it when. . . ") rather than paper summary. Procedures are skeletons the *shape* of the method, not full reproduction. Each step ends with "See paper for details." Knowledge-cards optimized for downstream agent to retrieve and act on, not for human reading. They look like cookbook recipe cards keyed by paper. Skill cards compiled from research papers on data curation vision-language models (VLMs) ## Characteristics of the skills Four top-level buckets, each stage of the VLM data pipeline: data-acquisition, data-curation, data-selection, data-synthesis Each skill is one folder containing exactly one file: SKILL.md with title that combines the papers short title and source. 1 paper -> 1 skill. No multi-paper aggregations; no extra assets (no code, no figures, no PDFs). **Per-file structure (rigidly templated)** **Tone / philosophy** Decision-first ("Use this skill when. . . " / "Avoid it when. . . ") rather than paper summary. Procedures are skeletons the *shape* of the method, not full reproduction. Each step ends with "See paper for details." Knowledge-cards optimized for downstream agent to retrieve and act on, not for human reading. They look like cookbook recipe cards keyed by paper. 34 Light-scaffolding I: General Data Strategists ## Curation strategies to explore Ideas (not limited to these): **Subset balancing**: Analyze source_subset distribution and adjust proportions. **Quality filtering**: Score by text quality (length, complexity, vocabulary diversity). **Diversity sampling**: Cover diverse image types and question categories. **Task-type targeting**: Prioritize samples matching eval benchmarks (OCR, visual reasoning, open-ended QA). **Deduplication**: Remove near-duplicates to make room for diverse samples. **Difficulty scoring**: Balanced mix of easy/medium/hard. **Conversation structure**: Prioritize multi-turn or specific conversation patterns. **Image analysis**: Use image metadata (resolution, content type) to guide selection. Heavy-scaffolding: Self-research (rewriting) Mandatory LLM rewriting after baselines: Every post-baseline iteration must apply the MLLM provided as tool to rewrite at least part of the training set (>20% samples recommended, >50% encouraged). Selection-only or programmatic-only iterations are explicitly banned. Research-first mandate with eval-row grounding: Similar to "self-research scaffolds", this version requires writing research plan at each iteration, requiring the observation to cite specific failure rows from raw evaluation outputs (question, model output, gold answer), not just aggregate scores. research plan that only references aggregated scores is explicitly declared insufficient. Rewrite-recipe research questions: set of examples is provided as starting points: answer-length mismatches, format gaps, malformed samples, turn restructuring, ambiguous questions. These frame the kind of hypotheses the agent should form, distinct from previous selection-oriented thinking. Rewrite verification step: After curation/rewriting but before training, the agent must eyeball 510 rewritten samples to confirm the transform actually did what the hypothesis predicted. If the rewrite is generic or didnt change what was expected, the agent must fix the recipe before burning training run. The result logs gains rewrite recipe column describing the specific LLM rewrite applied (target slice + transform). Failure analysis section: Requires reading raw evaluation files after scoring sampling 515 wrong rows per benchmark, categorizing failures (OCR error, format violation, hallucinated option, etc.), and cross-referencing with training data to find the linkage between what the model learned and where it fails. Revised no-contamination rule: An explicit "No data contamination" rule forbids using evaluation questions or answers (from eval logs) to construct training examples not as copies, paraphrases, or rewrite targets mirroring specific eval items. Patterns are fair game; specific items are not. Adapt papers: EL2N top-loss with p95 assistant-loss noise filter (removes top 5% noisiest) Data curation: ShareGPT4V-inspired response quality scoring. Skill: sharegpt4v-improving-large-multi-modal-models-with-better-captions-arxiv-2311-12793v2 Adaptation: ShareGPT4V improves VLM training by ensuring high-quality captions/responses. We adapt this by decomposing the base models forward-pass loss into total loss (EL2N informativeness) and assistant-only loss (response coherence quality). Select samples with high total loss (informative) but NOT extreme assistant-only loss (coherent responses). This filters out high-loss samples whose difficulty comes from noisy/incoherent responses rather than genuinely complex visual understanding.
